grammarLessons.name: 할 수 있어요 — Can / Able To

cards.grammarNote:
Professions are stated directly — no article needed.
**저는 의사예요** = I am a doctor.
The copula **이에요/예요** attaches to the noun: **이에요** after a consonant, **예요** after a vowel.

**간호사** (ganhosa) = nurse — Sino-Korean: **간호** (看護, care/nursing) + **사** (士, specialist).
The **-사** suffix marks many professional titles: **의사** (doctor), **변호사** (lawyer), **요리사** (cook).
**예요** = copula after a vowel: **간호사예요** = 'is a nurse'.

**엔지니어** (enjinieo) is a loanword from English — the most common term in modern Korean.
The native Sino-Korean term is **기술자** (gisulcha) = technician/engineer.
**오빠** (oppa) = older brother (used by females) — the term differs by gender of speaker.
**예요** = copula after a vowel ending: **엔지니어예요**.

**프로그래머** (peurogeuraeмео) is a loanword from English.
**로/으로** = 'as / in the role of': **프로그래머로 일해요** = 'work as a programmer'.
Use **으로** after a consonant and **로** after a vowel or ㄹ — **프로그래머** ends in a vowel, so **로**.
**일하다** (ilhada) = to work — a **하다** compound verb: **일** (work) + **하다** (to do).

**요리사** (yorisa) = cook/chef — **요리** (cooking) + **사** (士, specialist).
**주방** (jubang) = kitchen (formal/professional setting); **부엌** (bueok) = kitchen (home setting).
**에서** after a place = where an action takes place: **주방에서 일해요** = 'works in the kitchen'.
**요리** also means 'cuisine/dish' — context clarifies: **한국 요리** (Korean cuisine), **요리사** (the cook).
cards.englishPhrase: The cook works in the kitchen.

cards.grammarNote:
**웨이터** (weiteo) is a loanword — common for upscale restaurants.
In everyday Korean eateries, use **직원** (jigwon, staff) or call out **저기요!** (jeogiyo, excuse me!).
**가** = subject marker after a vowel: **웨이터가**.
**가져오다** = to bring: **가** (go) + **져** + **오다** (come) — come bringing something.

**매니저** (maeniжео) is a loanword from English — used in business and entertainment.
The Sino-Korean equivalent is **관리자** (gwallija) = administrator/manager.
**예요** = copula after a vowel: **매니저예요** = 'is a manager'.
**그녀** (geunyeo) = 'she' — a pronoun used mainly in writing; in speech, Koreans often omit the pronoun when context is clear.

**학생** (haksaeng) = student — Sino-Korean: **학** (學, study/learn) + **생** (生, person/born).
**이에요** = copula after a consonant: **학생이에요** = 'is a student'.
**대학생** (daehaksaeng) = university student; **고등학생** = high school student; **중학생** = middle school student.
Students are addressed respectfully in Korean — even a young student receives **씨** in formal contexts.

**건축가** (geonchukga) = architect — Sino-Korean: **건축** (建築, construction/architecture) + **가** (家, expert/specialist).
The **-가** suffix marks artistic or intellectual experts: **음악가** (musician), **작가** (writer), **화가** (painter).
Contrast with **-사** (士) used for licensed professionals: **의사**, **변호사**.
**예요** = copula after a vowel: **건축가예요**.

**변호사** (byeonhosa) = lawyer — Sino-Korean: **변호** (辯護, defense/advocacy) + **사** (士, specialist).
**언니** (eonni) = older sister (used by females); **누나** (nuna) = older sister (used by males).
In Korean, sibling terms depend on both the older sibling's gender AND the speaker's gender.
**예요** = copula after a vowel: **변호사예요**.

**야구** (yagu) = baseball — Sino-Korean: **야** (野, field) + **구** (球, ball).
Baseball is one of Korea's most popular sports — Korean pro league is **KBO**.
**보는 걸** = 'watching (as an object)': **보다** (watch) nominalised with **-는 것** → contracted to **-는 걸**.
**좋아하다** = to like: **보는 걸 좋아해요** = 'like watching'.

**은퇴자** (euntoeja) = retired person: **은퇴** (隱退, retirement) + **자** (者, person).
**은퇴하다** (euntoehada) = to retire — a **하다** verb.
**아버지** (abeoji) = father (formal); **아빠** (appa) = dad (casual/affectionate).
**은퇴했어요** = 'has retired' — past tense expressing a completed state change.

**독서** (dokseo) = reading (as a hobby) — Sino-Korean: **독** (讀, read) + **서** (書, book).
**책을 읽다** (chaegeul ikda) = to read a book — the verb-based phrase, more common in speech.
**독서를 좋아해요** = 'like reading' — **를** = object marker after a vowel.
**독서실** (dokseosil) = study room — a very common paid quiet-study space in Korea.

**글쓰기** (geulsseuggi) = writing — compound: **글** (text/writing) + **쓰기** (the act of writing, from **쓰다**).
**글** = any piece of writing; **글쓰기** = the activity/hobby of writing.
**이야기 쓰는 걸 좋아해요** = 'likes writing stories' — **쓰는 걸** nominalises the action.
**-기** and **-는 것/걸** both nominalise verbs; **-는 걸** is more common with **좋아하다**.

**그림 그리기** (geurim geurigi) = painting/drawing — literally 'the drawing of pictures'.
**그림** (geurim) = picture/painting; **그리기** = the act of drawing (nominalised **그리다**).
**그림을 그리다** = to draw/paint a picture — **그리다** means both draw and paint.
**그리는 걸 좋아해요** = 'likes drawing' — nominalised with **-는 걸** for use with **좋아하다**.

**그림 그리기** (geurim geurigi) = drawing — identical word to 'painting' in Korean.
**스케치하다** (seukechiada) = to sketch — a loanword used for pencil/line drawing.
**그림 그리는 걸 좋아해요** = 'I like drawing' — **-는 걸** nominalises the action for use after **좋아하다**.
Korea has a strong webtoon (웹툰) culture — **웹툰 작가** = webtoon artist.

**사진 촬영** (sajin chwallyeong) = photography — **사진** (photograph) + **촬영** (shooting/filming).
**사진 찍기** (sajin jjikgi) = the act of taking photos — **찍다** (to take a photo/stamp).
**사진을 찍다** = to take a photo; **사진이 잘 나오다** = 'the photo came out well'.
**취미** (chwimi) = hobby: **취미예요** = 'it is my hobby'.

**음악** (eumak) = music — Sino-Korean: **음** (音, sound) + **악** (樂, music/pleasure).
**음악을 듣다** = to listen to music — **듣다** (to listen) is an irregular ㄷ verb: **들어요**.
**음악을 하다** = to play/make music (more general); **악기를 연주하다** = to play an instrument.
**매일** = every day — does not take the **에** particle.
cards.englishPhrase: I listen to music every day.

cards.grammarNote:
**노래** (norae) = song OR singing — the word covers both the noun (a song) and the activity.
**노래를 부르다** = to sing — **부르다** (to call/sing) is the specific verb for singing.
**노래하다** = to sing (as a verb, slightly more formal).
**노래방** (noraebangs) = karaoke room — **노래** (song) + **방** (room) — a quintessential Korean social activity.

The verb 'to play' varies by instrument type:
**치다** for string/keyboard: **기타를 쳐요** (I play guitar), **드럼을 쳐요** (I play drums).
**불다** for wind instruments: **플루트를 불어요** (I play flute).

**춤** (chum) = dance (the noun); **춤을 추다** = to dance — **추다** is the dedicated verb for dancing.
**춤추는 걸 좋아해요** = 'like dancing' — **-는 걸** nominalises the action for **좋아하다**.
**저희** (jeohui) = 'we/our' — the humble/formal form of **우리** (uri), used in polite contexts.
K-pop idol dance culture has made **댄스** (daenseu, dance loanword) equally common.

**요리** (yori) = cooking (noun) OR a cooked dish — **요리하다** (to cook) adds **하다**.
**요리하는 걸 좋아해요** = 'I enjoy cooking' — nominalised with **-하는 걸** for use with **좋아하다**.
**요리사** (yorisa) = cook/chef: **요리** + **사** (specialist suffix).
**집밥** (jipbap) = home cooking — one of the most culturally warm food words in Korean.

**원예** (wonye) = horticulture/gardening — Sino-Korean: **원** (園, garden) + **예** (藝, art/skill).
**텃밭 가꾸기** = vegetable garden tending — a common hobby phrase.
**힐링이 돼요** = 'becomes healing / is therapeutic' — **힐링** (healing loanword) + **되다** (to become).
**은** = topic marker after a consonant: **원예는** sets gardening as the topic.

**하이킹** (haiking) is a loanword from English.
**등산** (deungsan) = mountain hiking — native Sino-Korean: **등** (登, climb) + **산** (山, mountain). More common in Korean for serious hiking.
**에서** after a location = where an action takes place: **산에서 하이킹하는 걸** = 'hiking in the mountains'.
Korea is about 70% mountainous — hiking is one of the most popular national pastimes.
cards.englishPhrase: I like hiking in the mountains.

cards.grammarNote:
**달리기** (dalligi) = running (the activity) — **달리다** (to run) + **-기** (nominaliser).
**달려요** = 'run' (polite present) — the verb form of **달리다**.
**뛰다** (ttwida) = to jump/run — a more general verb; **달리다** is specifically for sustained running.
**매일 아침** = every morning — **매일** (every day) + **아침** (morning), no particle needed.

**조깅** (joging) is a loanword from English.
**조깅하다** = to jog — a **하다** compound: **조깅** + **하다**.
**에서** after a location = where an action takes place: **공원에서 조깅해요** = 'jogs in the park'.
**공원** (gongwon) = park — Sino-Korean: **공** (公, public) + **원** (園, garden).

**자전거** (jajeongeo) = bicycle — Sino-Korean: **자** (自, self) + **전** (轉, turn) + **거** (車, vehicle) — 'self-turning vehicle'.
**타다** (tada) = to ride — used for all vehicles: **자전거를 타다** (ride a bike), **버스를 타다** (take a bus).
**주말에** = 'on weekends' — **에** marks a recurring time point.
**타기** = the act of riding (nominalised).

**수영** (suyeong) = swimming — Sino-Korean: **수** (水, water) + **영** (泳, swim).
**수영하다** = to swim; **수영을 좋아해요** = 'like swimming' — **을** = object marker after a consonant.
**수영장** (suyeongjang) = swimming pool — **수영** + **장** (場, place/venue).
**수영복** (suyeongbok) = swimsuit — **수영** + **복** (服, clothing).

**요가** (yoga) is a loanword from Sanskrit/English.
**요가를 하다** = to do yoga — **하다** is used with many sport/activity loanwords.
**매일 아침** = every morning — time adverb compound, no particle needed.
**요가복** (yogabok) = yoga wear — **요가** + **복** (服, clothing).

**운동** (undong) = exercise/sports — Sino-Korean: **운** (運, move) + **동** (動, motion).
**운동하러** = 'in order to exercise' — **-하러** is the purposive form: going somewhere to do something.
**헬스장** (helseujanг) = gym/health club — **헬스** (health loanword) + **장** (場, place).
**운동하다** (to exercise) is a **하다** verb: **운동해요** (I exercise).
cards.englishPhrase: I go to the gym for exercise.

cards.grammarNote:
**여행** (yeohaeng) = travel — Sino-Korean: **여** (旅, journey) + **행** (行, go/travel).
**여행하다** = to travel; **여행을 가다** = to go on a trip.
**여행을 정말 좋아해요** = 'I really love traveling' — **을** = object marker after a consonant.
**여행사** (yeohaengsa) = travel agency — **여행** + **사** (社, company).

**캠핑** (kaemping) is a loanword from English — extremely popular in modern Korea.
**캠핑을 가다** = to go camping — **을** = object marker after a consonant.
**여름에** = 'in summer' — **여름** (summer) + **에** (time marker for seasons).
**글램핑** (geullaemping) = glamping — also common, mixing luxury with outdoor camping.
cards.englishPhrase: We go camping in the summer.

cards.grammarNote:
**게임** (geim) is a loanword from English — Korea is a global leader in esports and gaming culture.
**게임을 하다** = to play games — **하다** is used with activity loanwords.
**밤에** = 'at night' — **밤** (night) + **에** (time particle for specific times).
**PC방** (pissi bang) = PC café — **방** (room) — a uniquely Korean gaming social space.
cards.englishPhrase: He plays video games at night.

cards.grammarNote:
**TV 보기** = watching TV — **보기** is the nominalised form of **보다** (to watch/see).
**영화** (yeonghwa) = movie — Sino-Korean: **영** (映, reflect/project) + **화** (畵, picture).
**보는 걸 좋아해요** = 'like watching' — **-는 걸** nominalises **보다** for use with **좋아하다**.
**OTT** services like Netflix are called **OTT** or **온라인 동영상 서비스** in Korean.

**음악 듣기** = listening to music — **듣기** is the nominalised form of **듣다** (to listen).
**듣다** is an irregular ㄷ verb: the ㄷ changes to 르 before vowels — **들어요** (listen), **들었어요** (listened).
**듣는 걸 좋아해요** = 'likes listening' — **-는 걸** nominalises for use with **좋아하다**.
**K-pop** = **케이팝** — Korea's globally exported music genre.

**공예** (gongye) = crafts/handicrafts — Sino-Korean: **공** (工, work/craft) + **예** (藝, art/skill).
**공예품** (gongyepum) = craft item/handmade product — **공예** + **품** (品, item).
**하고 있어요** = 'is doing' — present progressive: **하다** + **-고 있다**.
**아이들이** — **들** = plural suffix; **이** = subject marker after a consonant.

**체스** (cheseu) is a loanword from English.
The Korean board game equivalent is **바둑** (baduk) = Go — a deeply traditional Korean strategy game.
**두다** (duda) = to play (board games) — the specific verb for chess, Go, and checkers.
**두세요?** = 'do you play?' — **-세요** is the honorific imperative/question form of **하다**-type verbs here.

**보드게임** (bodeugeim) is a loanword compound: **보드** (board) + **게임** (game).
**보드게임을 하다** = to play board games — **하다** is used with most game loanwords.
**주말에** = 'on weekends' — **에** marks a recurring time point.
**을** = object marker after a consonant: **보드게임을**.
cards.englishPhrase: We play board games on weekends.

cards.grammarNote:
**일하다** (ilhada) = to work — **일** (work) + **하다** (to do).
**에서** after a workplace = where an action takes place: **병원에서 일해요** = 'work at a hospital'.
**로/으로** after a job = 'as a': **의사로 일해요** = 'work as a doctor'.
**일** alone = work (noun): **일이 많아요** (there is a lot of work).

**벌다** (beolda) = to earn (money) — used specifically for earning income.
**월급을 받다** (wolgebeul batda) = to receive a salary — **받다** (receive) is actually more natural than **벌다** in this context.
**월급** (wolgeup) = monthly salary — Sino-Korean: **월** (月, month) + **급** (給, pay).
**잘** = well: **잘 받아요** = 'receives well / earns well'.

**은퇴하다** (euntoehada) = to retire — **은퇴** (隱退, stepping back/retiring) + **하다**.
**작년** (jangnyeon) = last year — Sino-Korean: **작** (昨, last/previous) + **년** (年, year).
**에** after a time period = 'in/during': **작년에** = 'last year'.
**은퇴했어요** = 'retired' — past tense of **은퇴하다**.

**직업** (jigeoб) = job/occupation — Sino-Korean: **직** (職, position/duty) + **업** (業, work/business).
**있다** (itda) = to exist/have — **직업이 있다** = 'a job exists' = 'I have a job'.
**좋은 직업이 있어요** = 'I have a good job' — **이** = subject marker after a consonant.
**직업** is more formal than **일** (work); use **직업이 뭐예요?** = 'what is your occupation?'

**실직** (siljik) = unemployment/job loss — Sino-Korean: **실** (失, lose) + **직** (職, position).
**상태** (sangtae) = state/condition — **실직 상태** = 'state of being unemployed'.
**현재** (hyeonjae) = currently/at present — Sino-Korean: **현** (現, present) + **재** (在, exist).
**취업** (chwieop) = employment/getting a job — the positive counterpart: **취업했어요** = 'got a job'.

**공부하다** (gongbuhada) = to study — **공부** (工夫/工夫, study effort) + **하다**.
**공부해요** = polite present form — **하다** verbs follow the **해요** pattern.
**공부** alone = studying (noun): **공부가 힘들어요** = 'studying is hard'.
**매일 공부해요** = 'I study every day' — **매일** = every day (no particle needed).

**배우다** (baeuda) = to learn — native Korean verb.
**공부하다** = to study (emphasises effort); **배우다** = to learn (emphasises acquisition).
**배우고 있어요** = 'am learning' — present progressive: **배우다** + **-고 있다**.
**한국어** (hangugeo) = Korean language — **를** = object marker after a vowel.

**연습하다** (yeonseuphada) = to practice — Sino-Korean: **연습** (練習, practice/drill) + **하다**.
**연습** alone is the noun: **연습이 필요해요** = 'practice is needed'.
**매일 연습해요** = 'I practice every day' — **매일** stands alone without a particle.
**연습 문제** (yeonseup munje) = practice problem — common in Korean study materials.

**가르치다** (gareuchida) = to teach — native Korean verb.
Do not confuse with **가르키다** (garukkida) = to point at — they sound similar but mean different things.
**가르쳐요** = polite present form of **가르치다**.
**선생님** (seonsaengnim) = teacher — the **-님** suffix adds honorific respect.

**짓다** (jitda) = to build (structures) OR to cook rice — the same verb for both!
**집을 짓다** = to build a house; **밥을 짓다** = to cook/make rice.
Irregular ㅅ verb: the ㅅ drops before vowels — **지어요** (build), **지었어요** (built).
**짓고 있어요** = 'is being built' — present progressive.
cards.englishPhrase: They are building a new school.

cards.grammarNote:
**고치다** (gochida) = to fix/repair — also means to correct (mistakes, habits).
**수리하다** (surihada) = to repair — more technical, used for machines and appliances.
**고칠 수 있어요?** = 'can you fix this?' — **-ㄹ 수 있다** = 'can / be able to'.
**이거** = this (casual, contracted from **이것** = this thing).

**만들다** (mandeulda) = to make/create — an irregular ㄹ verb: the ㄹ drops before consonants.
**만들어요** = polite present; **만들었어요** = past tense.
**만든** = past modifier form: **집에서 만든 빵** (homemade bread).
**커피를 만들다** = to make coffee — **를** = object marker after a vowel.

**디자인하다** (dijainhada) = to design — **디자인** (design loanword) + **하다**.
**디자인** alone = design (noun): **디자인이 예뻐요** = 'the design is pretty'.
**웹사이트** (wepsaiteu) = website — loanword from English.
**그래픽 디자이너** (graepik dijaino) = graphic designer — a common loanword profession title.

**팔다** (palda) = to sell — an irregular ㄹ verb: the ㄹ drops before ㄴ/ㅂ/시.
**팔아요** = polite present; **팔았어요** = past tense.
**자동차** (jadongcha) = car — Sino-Korean: **자** (自, self) + **동** (動, move) + **차** (車, vehicle).
Opposite: **사다** (sada) = to buy.

**사다** (sada) = to buy — a simple vowel-stem verb.
**사야 해요** = 'must buy' — **-아야 하다** expresses obligation: **사다** → **사야 해요**.
**컴퓨터** (keompyuteo) = computer — loanword.
**새** (sae) = new (used only as a modifier before nouns, not as a predicate); **새 컴퓨터** = new computer.
cards.englishPhrase: I need to buy a new computer.

cards.grammarNote:
**돕다** (dopda) = to help — irregular ㅂ verb: **도와요** (help), **도왔어요** (helped).
**도와드릴까요?** = 'shall I help you?' — **도와드리다** is the humble/honorific form: 'help (upward)'.
**드리다** (deurida) = humble form of **주다** (give) — used when giving/offering to a superior.
Compare: **도와줄까요?** (shall I help you? — equal level) vs. **도와드릴까요?** (shall I help you? — respectful).

**관리하다** (gwallihada) = to manage/administer — Sino-Korean: **관리** (管理, management) + **하다**.
**관리** alone = management (noun): **관리가 필요해요** = 'management is needed'.
**팀을 관리하다** = to manage a team — **을** = object marker after a consonant.
**관리자** (gwallija) = manager/administrator — the person who does **관리**.

**만나다** (mannada) = to meet — a simple vowel-stem verb: **만나요** (meet), **만났어요** (met).
**만남** (mannam) = a meeting/encounter — the noun form of **만나다**.
**매주** (maeju) = every week — **매주 월요일에** = 'every Monday'.
**처음 뵙겠습니다** = 'nice to meet you (for the first time)' — the formal first-meeting phrase.

**시작하다** (sijaghada) = to start/begin — **시작** (始作, start) + **하다**.
**시작하다** is transitive (takes an object): **일을 시작해요** = 'start work'.
**시작되다** (sijakdoeda) = to begin (intransitive/passive): **회의가 시작돼요** = 'the meeting starts'.
**여덟 시에** = 'at eight o'clock' — native Korean number **여덟** (eight) + **시** (時, o'clock) + **에**.

**끝나다** (kkeunnada) = to end/finish (intransitive) — 'the work ends'.
**끝내다** (kkeunnaeda) = to finish/complete (transitive) — 'I finish the work'.
**일이 끝나요** = 'work ends' — **이** = subject marker after a consonant.
**퇴근하다** (toegeunhada) = to leave work — a more specific and commonly used workplace verb.

**좋아하다** = to like.
Liking a thing: **X를/을 좋아해요** (I like X).
Liking an activity: **X하는 걸 좋아해요** (I like doing X).
**즐기다** = to enjoy (implies stronger, more active enjoyment).
cards.englishPhrase: I like reading. She likes singing.

cards.grammarNote:
**쉬다** (swida) = to rest/relax — a basic, essential verb.
**쉬어요** = polite present; **쉬었어요** = past tense.
**휴식을 취하다** (hyusigeul chwihada) = to take a rest — more formal expression.
**주말에 쉬어요** = 'I rest on weekends' — **에** after **주말** marks a recurring time.

**시간을 보내다** (siganeul bonaeda) = to spend time — **시간** (time) + **을** (obj) + **보내다** (send/spend).
**보내다** also means 'to send': **메시지를 보내다** (send a message) — context clarifies.
**에** after an activity = 'on': **독서에 시간을 보내요** = 'spend time on reading'.
**많은 시간** = a lot of time — adjective modifier form of **많다**.
cards.englishPhrase: I spend a lot of time reading.

cards.grammarNote:
**창의적이다** (changruijeogida) = to be creative — **창의** (創意, creative idea) + **적이다** (-적 = adjectival suffix).
**-적이다** turns Sino-Korean nouns into adjectives: **창의적** (creative), **효율적** (efficient), **전통적** (traditional).
**창의적이에요** = polite present; **창의적인** = modifier form before nouns.
**매우** (maeu) = very — a formal intensifier; **너무** is more casual.

**흥미롭다** (heungmiropda) = to be interesting — irregular ㅂ verb: **흥미로워요** in speech.
**흥미** (heungmi) = interest — Sino-Korean: **흥** (興, excitement) + **미** (味, taste/flavor).
**흥미로운** = modifier form before nouns: **흥미로운 일** (interesting work).
**재미있다** (jaemiitda) = to be fun/interesting (more casual, about enjoyment).

**스트레스** (seuteuresse) is a loanword from English.
**스트레스가 많다** = 'there is a lot of stress' — **가** = subject marker after a vowel.
**스트레스를 받다** = 'to receive/feel stress' — a common fixed expression.
**스트레스가 많은** = modifier form: **스트레스가 많은 일** (stressful work).

**지루하다** (jiruhada) = to be boring/tedious — a **하다** adjective.
**지루한** = modifier form before nouns: **지루한 회의** (boring meeting).
**재미없다** (jaemieopda) = to be uninteresting/boring — literally 'fun doesn't exist'.
**지루하다** implies something drags on; **재미없다** simply means it isn't enjoyable.

**월급** (wolgeup) = monthly salary — Sino-Korean: **월** (月, month) + **급** (給, pay/give).
**연봉** (yeonbong) = annual salary — **연** (年, year) + **봉** (俸, stipend).
**월급이 얼마예요?** = 'what is the salary?' — **이** = subject marker after a consonant.
**월급날** (wolgeupnal) = payday — a beloved compound: **월급** + **날** (day).

**일정** (iljeong) = schedule/itinerary — Sino-Korean: **일** (日, day) + **정** (程, extent/program).
**스케줄** (seukejeul) is a loanword alternative — both are common in modern Korean.
**유연하다** (yuyeonhada) = to be flexible — **유연한 일정** (flexible schedule).
**제 일정은** — **제** = my (polite); **은** = topic marker after a consonant.

**휴가** (hyuga) = vacation/leave — Sino-Korean: **휴** (休, rest) + **가** (暇, free time).
**휴가를 가다** = to go on vacation — the standard expression.
**연차** (yeoncha) = annual leave (paid time off from work).
**다음 주에** = 'next week' — **다음** (next) + **주** (week) + **에** (time particle).
cards.englishPhrase: I'm going on vacation next week.

cards.grammarNote:
**휴식** (hyusik) = rest/break — Sino-Korean: **휴** (休, rest) + **식** (息, breath/pause).
**휴식을 취하다** = 'to take a rest' — the formal compound expression.
**잠깐 쉬어요** = 'let's rest briefly' — **잠깐** (briefly/a moment) + **쉬다** (to rest).
**쉬다** (swida) is the everyday verb; **휴식** is the more formal noun.

**사무실** (samusil) = office — Sino-Korean: **사무** (事務, office affairs) + **실** (室, room).
**회사** (hoesa) = company — the broader workplace; **사무실** = the specific office room.
**에서** after a workplace = location of action: **사무실에서 일해요** = 'work in an office'.
**재택근무** (jaetaekgeunmu) = working from home — a very topical modern compound.

**야외** (yaoe) = outdoors/open air — Sino-Korean: **야** (野, field/outdoors) + **외** (外, outside).
**야외 활동** = outdoor activities — **활동** (活動, activity).
**에서** after a location = where an action takes place: **야외에서 일해요** = 'works outdoors'.
Contrast: **실내** (silnae) = indoors — **실** (室, room) + **내** (內, inside).

**면접** (myeonjeop) = job interview — Sino-Korean: **면** (面, face) + **접** (接, meet/receive).
**취업 면접** = job interview — **취업** (就業, employment).
**면접을 보다** = to have/sit a job interview — the standard fixed expression.
**면접관** (myeonjeokgwan) = interviewer; **면접자** (myeonjeopja) = interviewee.
cards.englishPhrase: I have a job interview tomorrow.

cards.grammarNote:
**동료** (dongnyo) = colleague/peer — Sino-Korean: **동** (同, same) + **료** (僚, official/colleague).
**직장 동료** = coworker — **직장** (職場, workplace).
**들** = plural suffix: **동료들** = colleagues.
**친하다** (chinhada) = to be close/friendly — **친한 동료** = a close colleague.

**고객** (gogaek) = customer/client — Sino-Korean: **고** (顧, look after) + **객** (客, guest).
**손님** (sonnim) = customer/guest — native Korean, more warmly used in shops and restaurants.
**고객 서비스** = customer service — **서비스** (service loanword).
**만족하다** (manjokada) = to be satisfied — **만족해요** = 'is satisfied'.

**팀** (tim) is a loanword from English — universally used in Korean workplaces.
**팀원** (timwon) = team member — **팀** + **원** (員, member).
**팀장** (timjang) = team leader — **팀** + **장** (長, head/leader).
**에서** after a group = where one belongs/acts: **훌륭한 팀에서 일해요** = 'work in a great team'.
cards.englishPhrase: I work in a great team.

cards.grammarNote:
**프로젝트** (peurojekteu) is a loanword from English — standard in Korean workplaces.
**프로젝트가** — **가** = subject marker after a vowel.
**거의 다 끝났어요** = 'is almost finished' — **거의** (almost) + **다** (all/completely) + **끝나다** (to finish) in past tense.
**업무** (eommu) = work/duties — a more formal synonym for **일** in business contexts.

**회의** (hoeui) = meeting/conference — Sino-Korean: **회** (會, gather) + **의** (議, discuss).
**회의가 있어요** = 'there is a meeting / I have a meeting' — **있다** handles both existence and possession.
**회의실** (hoeuisil) = conference room — **회의** + **실** (室, room).
**회의를 하다** = to hold a meeting; **회의에 참석하다** = to attend a meeting.
cards.englishPhrase: We have a meeting at ten.

cards.grammarNote:
**자유 시간** (jayu sigan) = free time — **자유** (自由, freedom) + **시간** (時間, time).
**여가 시간** (yeoga sigan) = leisure time — **여가** (餘暇, leisure) is also common.
**여가 시간에 뭐 해요?** = 'what do you do in your free time?' — **에** = 'during'.
**취미가 뭐예요?** = 'what is your hobby?' — a very common Korean conversation opener.
cards.englishPhrase: What do you do in your free time?
